question.text: Why is time stamping mandatory for all mutual fund transactions?

answer.text: To calculate the expense ratio applicable to the transaction
To verify the identity of the investor
To determine the applicable NAV based on the cut-off timing
To comply with income tax reporting requirements

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Time stamping records the exact time a transaction request is received. This is critical for determining which NAV applies — same-day or next-day — based on the cut-off times. Without accurate time stamping, there would be disputes about which NAV should be applied to a transaction.
**The Trap:** Time stamping is not just about record-keeping. It has a direct financial impact on the investor since the applicable NAV determines the number of units allotted (purchase) or the redemption proceeds. A few minutes' difference around cut-off can mean a different NAV.
**Cross-Exam Context:** SEBI mandates time stamping at the point of receipt — whether at the AMC office, RTA office, distributor location, or online. All collection points must have mechanisms for accurate time stamping.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Maintain proper time-stamping records at your office if you accept physical applications. In case of any dispute about the applicable NAV, the time stamp on the application form at the point of first receipt will be the determining factor.
